attributes.stem:
A 38-year-old man is seen in the gastroenterology clinic with a three month history of fatigue, right upper quadrant abdominal pain and intermittent jaundice. His only other health issue is ulcerative colitis and his has been maintained on mesalazine therapy for three years. He does not smoke and drinks about six glasses of wine at weekends.

On examination his blood pressure is 116/72 mmHg, pulse 84/min and temperature 37.4°C. His liver is palpable 2 cm below the costal margin and the rest of the physical examination is unremarkable. Notable laboratory investigations include a serum bilirubin of 54 µmol/L (4–20) and serum alkaline phosphatase of 430 U/L (30–110).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following investigations would be most appropriate?
attributes.explanation:
Given the patient's history of mesalamine therapy indicating ulcerative colitis in this patient and the presence of hepatomegaly, elevated bilirubin, and alkaline phosphatase, the most likely diagnosis is primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC). PSC is a chronic inflammatory condition characterised by inflammation and fibrosis of the bile ducts, leading to bile duct strictures and liver dysfunction. Around 60%-80% of PSC patients also have concurrent inflammatory bowel disease, most commonly ulcerative colitis (UC). The diagnosis of PSC depends on clinical, laboratory, and imaging with magnetic resonance cholangiography (MRC). The main diagnostic features include elevated alkaline phosphatase (ALP) levels for more than six months, characteristic findings on imaging studies and exclusion of secondary causes such as immunoglobulin G4-related sclerosing disease, recurrent pyogenic cholangitis, ischaemic cholangitis, acquired immunodeficiency syndrome-related cholangitis, and eosinophilic cholangitis. MRC is a non-invasive imaging technique that uses magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) to visualise the bile ducts. MRC can reveal characteristic features of PSC, such as multifocal strictures, dilatations, and beading of the intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts. It is beneficial for detecting bile duct abnormalities without the invasiveness of ERCP.

Endoscopic retrograde cholangiography (ERC) involves passing an endoscope through the mouth and into the duodenum to inject contrast directly into the bile ducts. It is considered an invasive procedure but is highly effective in visualising the bile ducts and detecting abnormalities such as strictures, dilatations, and beading characteristic of PSC. ERCP is often used to confirm the diagnosis of PSC and assess the extent of bile duct involvement. Since the diagnostic accuracy of MRC is equivalent to ERCP, the latter is not the first choice since it is invasive.

Liver biopsy entails obtaining a small sample of liver tissue for microscopic examination. While not typically the initial diagnostic modality for PSC, it can be valuable in confirming the diagnosis and assessing the degree of liver damage. Histopathological findings characteristic of PSC may include periductal onion skin fibrosis, though this feature is not present in all cases. Liver biopsy may also help differentiate PSC from other liver diseases and assess the degree of liver inflammation and fibrosis.

The patient's current symptoms and abnormal laboratory findings (elevated bilirubin and alkaline phosphatase) indicate a hepatobiliary issue rather than a gastrointestinal one. While colonoscopy is essential for monitoring ulcerative colitis, it is not directly useful in diagnosing the cause of jaundice and liver enzyme abnormalities. It will not provide information about the bile ducts or liver pathology, the immediate concerns given the clinical picture.

Percutaneous transhepatic cholangiography (PTC) involves inserting a needle through the skin into the liver to inject contrast directly into the bile ducts. It is typically reserved for cases where ERCP is not feasible or when additional information is needed following inconclusive imaging studies. While effective in visualising the bile ducts, PTC is more invasive than MRCP and ERCP and is not usually the first-line diagnostic modality for PSC.

In summary, while all options may have some role in diagnosing or managing PSC, the most appropriate diagnostic modality is typically magnetic resonance cholangiography (MRC) due to its non-invasive nature and ability to visualise characteristic bile duct abnormalities associated with PSC.
learningPoints: Primary sclerosing cholangitis is a chronic, progressive liver disease characterised by inflammation, fibrosis, and strictures of the bile ducts, leading to cholestasis and liver dysfunction.
Primary sclerosing cholangitis is often associated with inflammatory bowel disease, particularly ulcerative colitis.
references: Seo N, Kim SY, Lee SS, Byun JH, Kim JH, Kim HJ, Lee MG. Sclerosing Cholangitis: Clinicopathologic Features, Imaging Spectrum, and Systemic Approach to Differential Diagnosis. Korean J Radiol. 2016 Jan-Feb;17(1):25-38. doi: 10.3348/kjr.2016.17.1.25. Epub 2016 Jan 6. PMID: 26798213; PMCID: PMC4720808.
Dyson JK, Beuers U, Jones DEJ et al. Primary sclerosing cholangitis. Lancet 2018; 391: 2547–59
